Indexical Space — All Signs

Each point is one sign. Click any point for details. Quadrant thresholds at 45 (x) and 55 (y) reveal typological patterns of neighborhood fragility.

Resilient Vernacular — low resources, high accessibility

Compound Fragility — low resources, low accessibility

District Comparison

Average First-Order and Second-Order scores per district.

Who Feels Welcome?

Welcome distribution by district (% of coded signs).

Typographic Ecosystem

The neighborhood's signage read as a biological ecosystem — typographic diversity measured across economic registers, not just within them. Register emerges from the multiplication of Para-level (material substrate) and Micro-level (typographic classification).

Typographic Voice Count

How many distinct typographic classification choices are made per sign? This is the basic diversity measure at the sign level — combining unique typestyles, modulations, colours, backgrounds, and text/image choices.

Typographic Biodiversity Index Shannon

For each district: how many distinct typestyle categories appear and how evenly distributed are they? Borrowed from ecology. High Shannon + low material register variety = monoculture masquerading as diversity. High Shannon + cross-register variety = genuine ecosystem.

Para × Micro Cross-Tabulation

For each district: which Material × Typestyle combinations dominate? A single pair appearing far more than others is a monoculture signal. Spread across both high-investment and low-investment materials indicates cross-register diversity.

Register Divergence 66 flagged

Signs where the welcome score diverges from what the Para × Micro combination would predict (Δ ≥ 1.5). These are the analytically interesting cases — where community knowledge overrides or undermines semiotic signals.

Click a pattern to explore all signs within it. Thresholds: First-Order <45 = low resources; >55 = high resources. Second-Order <45 = low accessibility; >55 = high accessibility.

Welcome Divergence Analysis

Comparing predicted welcome (from semiotic scores) vs. actual participant responses. Divergences of more than 25 points reveal where formal analysis misses lived community experience.

Typography Score vs. Upward Mobility

First- and Second-Order scores for signs geocoded to each tract, plotted against kfr p25 (income rank at 35 for children with parents at p25 nationally). National average = 45th percentile.

Racial Mobility Gap by Tract

White vs. Black upward mobility (kfr p25) for the three target tracts. The gap between groups within the same neighbourhood reveals place-based racial inequality.

Interpretation

Chetty Mobility Framework — Reference

Key variables from Opportunity Insights and their relationship to typographic indexicality.

Mobility Predictors
kfr p25Income rank at 35 · proxied by 2nd-Order score (social openness)
ec_zipEconomic connectedness · proxied by multilingual + cross-class genre mix
Poverty rateMaterial constraint · proxied by 1st-Order score
Single-parent rateFamily stability · cross-referenced with welcome scores
Your Typography Proxies
First-Order scoreMaterial investment → economic resource base
Second-Order scoreSocial accessibility → cross-class exposure potential
Linguistic diversityMultilingual signs → social bridge-building
Resilient VernacularHigh 2nd-Order despite low 1st → collective efficacy
Price Hill context: All three tracts sit 10–15 percentile points below the national upward mobility average (45th pctile). Tract 94 is the most distressed: poverty rate doubled from 28% (2000) to 53% (2010), college attainment of only 3.5%, and the lowest economic connectedness score (ec = 0.40) of any ZIP in the study area — well below the Hamilton County average of 0.70. The racial mobility gap (White vs. Black kfr) ranges from 3–7 percentile points within the same tracts.
Indexical Pattern

Scored Data (CSV)

All 273 signs with computed First-Order, Second-Order, and pattern classification scores.

Methods Statement

I developed a multi-dimensional indexical scoring system analyzing 273 typographic signs across five districts in Price Hill, Cincinnati. Each sign received numerical scores on two dimensions:


First-Order Score (0–100) — Material Resources: Computed as the mean of three sub-scores: material durability (highest-durability material per sign, e.g., Paper=10, Metal=70, Neon=90), making investment (highest-skill fabrication mode, e.g., Handwritten=15, Printed=60, Chiseled=83), and scale/visibility (largest size dimension, e.g., Tiny=0, Large=67, Huge=100).


Second-Order Score (0–100) — Social Accessibility: Computed as the mean of four sub-scores: linguistic accessibility (monolingual=33, bilingual=67, trilingual=100), genre accessibility (coded 15–95 by legibility/universality of use context), visual accessibility (Image only=100, Text+image=67, Plain text=50, Complex typestyle=33), and discourse openness (Private=15, Commercial=85, Regulatory/Infrastructural=90–92).


Signs were classified into typological patterns using thresholds of 45 (First-Order) and 55 (Second-Order): Resilient Vernacular (low resources, high accessibility), Inclusive Investment (high resources, high accessibility), Compound Fragility (low resources, low accessibility), and Exclusive Investment (high resources, low accessibility).
